data.title: YETI: A Better Bargain As International Sales Rise (Upgrade)
data.description:
Amid a lopsided and choppy stock market this year, the best thing for investors to be is active market-watchers. Valuations and fundamental stories are changing rapidly, which makes it necessary for us to adjust our positions and monitor more closely.

YETI (YETI), the consumer products company that is best known for its lineup of hard coolers and drinkware, is a company on which I’m growing more positive. Since the start of the year, shares of YETI have fallen ~20%, interrupting a fierce rebound rally that began last year. While I don’t think the stock is necessarily a "Buy" yet, multiples are looking more appealing, especially amid recent improvements in sales.

I last wrote a "Sell" article on YETI in January, when the stock was sitting at $47 per share. Since then, a number of factors have moved in a positive direction for YETI. First and foremost, valuation multiples have collapsed ~25%, and especially when we look forward to FY27 estimates, the stock is trading at a reasonably compelling P/E. Second, recent sales trends have improved, notably in international markets. With all of this factored in, I’m raising YETI back up to a "Neutral" rating. I believe it’s time to add this stock back to our watch lists, though there are still uncertainties on the horizon that need clarity before I can be fully bullish on this name.

To me, at current share prices, YETI is a mixed bag of positives and negatives. On the bright side for this company:

  • YETI’s brand reach is extending into expansive white space, especially overseas. International is a huge opportunity for YETI, with revenue growing at a high teens pace and still a relatively small contribution to the overall company at ~20% of sales.
  • High margins for a consumer products company. YETI makes relatively heavy products in a quite competitive industry. Its high 50s/low 60s gross margin profile signals the substantial brand equity the company has built up and gives the company plenty of headroom to generate economies of scale.

At the same time, however, we have to remain cautious of several risks:

  • Stagnant U.S. sales. The company’s revenue in the United States is pretty flat. This may signal saturation in core markets plus competition from the likes of Stanley.
  • Tariff headwinds. The fate of Trump’s reciprocal tariffs remains a wild card for calendar 2026 and beyond. For the moment, tariffs are a substantial ~2 point headwind to the company’s gross and operating margins.
  • Macro concerns. Weak consumer spending may dent sales, particularly in wholesale channels where resellers may be wary to take on slow-moving inventory.

All in all, I think YETI remains a watch-and-wait stock. The stock’s -20% re-rating over the past few months combined with sales acceleration makes it more compelling, but not a screaming buy just yet. Add this stock to your watchlist, but don’t rush in to buy.

Q4 Recap

Let’s now go through YETI’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

YETI’s revenue grew 7% y/y to $583.7 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $582.4 million by a slight margin. We note that this is an acceleration versus 2% full-company growth in Q3 and a marked improvement as well relative to a 4% decline in Q2.

Still, some issues persisted. U.S. sales declined 1% y/y, in line with Q3. The company’s sales improvement was entirely from its international arm, where revenue grew 16% y/y and accelerated versus 14% y/y in Q3. We note that we should expect international sales to decelerate in FY26 as the company laps its launch in Japan in Q2 of FY25.

The core risk for investors to track in recent performance is the fact that U.S. retail is becoming very promotional. Brands are slicing prices to entice customers with deals. At the same time, wholesalers are wary of loading up on inventory for fear of slow-moving product. If channel sales are weak, in the long run this could lead to YETI products being less prominently placed on retail shelves.

Per CFO Michael McMullen’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In Drinkware, sales grew 6% to $380 million. As we have noted, 2025 was a challenging year for Drinkware, reflecting U.S. market dynamics and the impact of our supply chain transformation. That said, we consistently communicated our expectations for improvement in Q4, and we were pleased to see that come through our results.

Growth was driven by innovation, strong international demand and continued positive consumer response to our broad assortment in this category. In the U.S., Drinkware sales were flat year-over-year, despite a promotional market and continued cautious wholesale buying.

The issue here is that YETI may feel more competitive pressure to bring pricing down to stimulate sales, especially to wholesale partners. Meanwhile, margins are already on thin ice thanks to tariffs. As shown below, YETI’s gross margin in Q4 fell 180bps y/y to 58.4%. The company notes that it faced a -310bps headwind from tariffs, which was partially offset by the company’s price increases implemented in 2025.

Meanwhile, this was compounded by opex rising 10% y/y, faster than revenue growth. This led to operating margins falling 370bps y/y and operating income dollars falling 14% y/y. EPS also fell 8% y/y to $0.92, though this did still beat Street expectations of $0.88 with 5% upside.

To me, sluggish U.S. sales and a faltering operating margin are the core issues that YETI needs to solve before I can confidently buy into this stock.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

Of course, we acknowledge as well that amid uncertainties, YETI’s valuation has also fallen at a commensurate level. At current share prices near $38, YETI trades at a $2.85 billion market cap. After netting off the $188.3 million in cash against $73.5 million of debt on the company’s current balance sheet, its resulting enterprise value is $2.74 billion.

For FY26, YETI has guided to 6%-8% sales growth, $200-$225 million in FCF, and 12%-14% EPS growth to a midpoint of $2.80.

This positions YETI’s valuation multiples at:

  • 12.8x EV/FY26 FCF
  • 13.5x FY26 P/E

These cheaper multiples are a decent offset against YETI's operating risks. That said, we also think the recent market slide has created plenty of bargain-hunting opportunities, particularly in the tech/software space, so YETI's low multiples aren't exactly unique among small/mid-cap stocks in this market environment.

Add this stock to your watchlist, but remain on the sidelines for now.

data.title: Archer Aviation: Time To Load Up
data.description:
Despite all of the big opportunities for eVTOLs and air taxis, Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) has recently slumped back to multi-year lows. The company now has multiple paths to launching services beyond key FAA certification. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish, with the stock back to $5 after a bad capital raise contributed to the irrational downside.

Moving Beyond FAA

About a month ago, Archer’s partners in Texas, Florida, and New York were selected for the White House’s eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP), a major step toward bringing air taxis to market in the United States. The company announced expectations to begin operations with partners in those states by the 2H'26.

While eIPP brings a pathway to starting operations in the U.S. for Archer Aviation, the company doesn't have aircraft to really start any significant operations. Management hasn't had any official conversations about the eIPP program since the government announcement.

The FAA's eIPP program apparently approved proposals primarily from Archer Aviation, Joby Aviation (JOBY), and BETA Technologies (BETA). The FAA listed specific project details as the following examples in New York and Texas involving Archer.

Along with the program in Abu Dhabi and the defense programs, Archer Aviation has several pathways to launching services without a binary outcome waiting on FAA certification. As operations get off the ground in New York, Florida, or the UAE, the FAA certification becomes less of a worry.

The biggest issue in the short term is that Archer Aviation doesn't have the aircraft for any meaningful operations. Along with the Q4'25 earnings report, the company didn't discuss any updated plans on aircraft production. The company plans to ramp aircraft production to nearly 50 in 2026.

The company won't announce Q1 results for another month, until mid-May, so the market won't hear more details about the financial impacts of the eIPP deal for a while. The deal does reduce the risk of any FAA certification slip and likely allows Archer Aviation to go full speed ahead with aircraft production to meet both eIPP aircraft demand and the UAE. The company can't really verify air taxi operations in the Texas triangle without multiple aircraft operating daily.

While the company announced an order book reaching $6 billion towards the end of 2024, Archer Aviation has announced additional big deals like $500 million for 100 aircraft with Korean Air back in 2025. Management hasn't done a great job highlighting updated totals as the company gets close to launching production.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO Adam Goldstein only made this basic statement on the order book:

Our order book is in the billions with 7 of the world's largest airlines choosing to partner with us. Some new partners include Saudi Arabia's PIF and the Serbian government.

Not Far From Cash Value

As the stock falls, Archer Aviation gets closer to cash value. The company ended last year with $2 billion in cash, and the stock valuation is now close to only $4 billion.

While the capital raise at only $8 back in November was frustrating, an investor can now buy the stock with the strong balance sheet as an asset. Archer Aviation forecasts burning upwards of $160 million in Q1 on building out the business this year, so the cash balance will quickly be spent to build up operations.

The Q1'26 results will also start to flow through the Hawthorne Airport operations. Analysts forecast Q1 revenues of $1.7 million, with the company only reaching $15.0 million for the year.

The consensus forecast is for revenues of nearly $115 million in 2027, followed by a jump to $512 million in 2028. The big focus is where revenues go in the next few years, with key questions on the upcoming Q1'26 earnings focused on manufacturing ramp plans with UAE and eIPP.

Archer Aviation has announced general plans to ramp production to 50 aircraft annually. At $5 million an aircraft, the company would produce upwards of $150 million in sales on aircraft alone.

With Anduril as a partner in the military segment and a customer for the hybrid powertrain system on their Omen drone, one needs to note the recent capital raise proposal. The defense-tech company is working towards a $60 billion valuation with a new founding round targeted at raising $8 billion in capital at double the value from 2025.

Again, this is why the capital raise by Archer Aviation was so destructive. Capital appears happy to invest in defense tech and modern aviation without the company needing a down funding round.

Of course, the biggest risk is the FAA dragging out any official certification. Archer Aviation can only produce so much for the UAE, and the eIPP plans quickly stall without the company working towards a certified aircraft.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Archer Aviation is down to an enterprise value of only $2 billion, while the company has an order book previously identified as topping $6 billion. The company has too many opportunities lining up in air taxis around the globe and work in the defense sector to not find the stock appealing here.

data.title: Papa John's: Cheap Enough To Consider Despite Dividend Cut Risks
data.description:
Introduction

Papa John's (PZZA) is down significantly from the post-pandemic hype era, facing significant macro pressure from a weak consumer environment, especially in North America, forcing the company to adapt.

Given their turnaround potential, attractive valuation, and ongoing takeover offer that can even result in a bidding war, PZZA is rated Buy, as the risk-reward is now in the company's favor as the stock fell to some of their lowest levels since 2013.

Turnaround Advancing

PZZA's Q4 numbers were mixed, with a very slight beat on EPS despite a miss on revenue, with the free cash flow reaching $61.305 million in 2025 (vs. $34.15 million in 2024 and $116.44 million in 2023) - without including the hit and insurance proceeds related to the impact of natural disasters.

Throughout 2025, PZZA opened 279 new restaurants, of which 96 were in North America and 183 were international, while closing a total of 226, for a net addition of 53, with global system-wide restaurant sales up ~1% to $4.92 billion, while NA comparable sales dropped ~2% while international ones grew by ~5%.

As part of the company's transformation, PZZA now targets $60 million in supply chain "Back to Basics" cost savings alongside closing 200 restaurants in North America (out of 300 underperforming restaurants identified, the majority to be closed by the end of 2027), which is not something exclusive to them in this environment. Papa John's management also talked about this in more detail during their Q4 Earnings Call, highlighting that this transformation is going to take time and results won't be "pretty" overnight:

We established a plan to deliver at least $60 million of system-wide supply chain cost savings to our company and franchise restaurants without compromising the customer experience. We identified at least $25 million of noncustomer-facing corporate cost savings to be realized through 2027. And we ended the year meeting or exceeding our updated guidance targets while investing $21 million in supplemental marketing year-over-year to support our value proposition and our franchisees. As we work to build on this momentum, I am even more confident that Papa John's is well positioned for meaningful medium- and long-term growth and value creation than I was at this time last year. Still, our progress is just beginning and near-term performance is mixed as our transformation initiatives begin to take hold.

As part of their 2026 guidance, PZZA sees global system-wide restaurant sales flat to down in low-single-digits, with a continued drop in NA comparable sales (-2% to -4%) and an increase internationally (2% to 4%), as well as $70 million to $80 million in CAPEX compared to $64.7 million in 2025.

Recently, PZZA also announced that they're also offering oven-roasted sandwiches in their effort to expand beyond pizza, which is not the worst choice given the common ingredients between these products and potential to attract more people, but it's also "a statement about where our brand is headed," as one of their managers said, aiming at adapting to the dynamic QSR environment.

Financially, based on Papa John's latest report, we see an overall normal position for a company in this industry, with current assets below their current liabilities and a relatively high amount of debt, most of it being due in 2029 (~$413.75 million) and 2028 (~$272.26 million). Note, however, that these come with low rates, with the 2029 debt, for example, coming at a 3.875% rate, which can be a risk if they're forced to refinance in a weak environment, although 2029 is quite a lot of time for rates to improve.

For reference, these 2029 3.875% coupon notes currently trade at a 5.557% yield to maturity, and it was as high as 7.755% back in 2023. However, we see their net interest expenses dropping to $40.77 million in 2025 compared to $42.58 million in 2024, with PZZA expecting $35 million to $40 million in net interest expenses in 2026.

In 2025, PZZA paid $61.14 million in dividends, with the yield currently standing at 5.27%, which is not the best out there given the nearly 100% payout ratio. If their downsizing combined with the potential for more macro pressure for longer ends up hurting this cash flow (especially given the higher CAPEX expected in 2026), it wouldn't be impossible to see them cut the dividend. Again, as mentioned in other cases before, I wouldn't mind this as a long-term investor, but the market can definitely tax them significantly for it, as it would then turn into (mostly) a turnaround play.

Takeover Potential

About a month ago, PZZA got a new takeover offer, with Qatari-backed Irth Capital Management (which already owns ~10% of the company) offering to pay $47 per share, after Apollo Global (APO) withdrew their offer to buy them at $64 per share back in November - after Apollo + Irth together offered $60 back in June.

Although it would be baffling to see them accept this one, it wouldn't be impossible for them to present interest, which can start a bidding war that can potentially result in much better prices than today.

Still, this bid also places them in a tougher spot, as denying it can not only result in the price falling since speculative arbitrage demand would be gone, but it can also push Irth into selling their significant stake, adding a lot more pressure on an already weakened stock, putting the management in a rough position.

However, this interest still shows that there's value or, at the very least, "fixable" potential here, while an acquisition can deliver interest savings through refinancing debt at better rates, taking out the dividend, and executing a more aggressive turnaround. I believe $47 is a lowball on purpose, probably hoping that the Iran conflict and its potential ripple effect would've forced the management's hand into negotiating from this level and likely getting a better deal.

Macro Pressure May Last for Longer

Right now, probably the main factor to watch is the ongoing Iran conflict - regardless of the recent ceasefire news. This is very important even for a company like PZZA, as we're talking about significant impacts on pump prices (hurting discretionary budgets even more) and near-term inflation that can pressure central banks into pausing rate cuts or even hiking in the near future.

Since PZZA has a relatively high amount of debt and their equity is also quite expensive given the dividend yield, their financial flexibility is thus reduced significantly, especially since we're talking about falling market shares - even though the company is still fighting.

This comes at a time when we're already in a weak environment for the industry, with very few players in the fast food and especially pizza industry (e.g., McDonald's (MCD) and Domino's (DPZ) are among the very few that are still doing fine) as a result of the falling discretionary budgets, while smaller peers like PZZA are being pressured even more, as they don't have the kind of supply chain and power of negotiation to offset these issues.

In such an environment, where the K-shaped economic discrepancy is growing, the bang for the buck plays a major role, and this is something that goes well beyond the taste of the product. Especially in a world that's going to be more and more dominated by digital orders, brand loyalty fades even more, as pizza's home delivery advantage is far from a moat anymore.

To briefly mention the GLP-1 threat since I see that it's a growing concern for the market, I'll reiterate that this is likely not that significant, and factors such as the competition or economic weakness are much more impactful. Very recently, we saw Morgan Stanley increasing their estimated adoption rate to ~15% of the US population by 2035, and if we assume a massive 10% hit in pizza consumption from these people (note: there's not enough data so far to estimate the specific impact on pizza, but a commonly referenced study sees a ~5.5% impact in general grocery spending and a low-double-digit hit for sweet bakery and snacks), we're still looking at a very modest impact on the company's volumes over an entire decade.

Although PZZA's management didn't talk about it, we have a very good quote from Domino's management during their Q4 Earnings Call, highlighting how this industry can also adapt to ongoing trends (e.g., higher protein, lower calories, etc.):

Yes, so on GLP-1, obviously, we continue to watch that closely. We have not seen an impact on our business so far. Obviously, with it coming out in pill form, we're going to wait and see if there's any implication there. Right now, though, when you read the literature on GLP-1s, it's really more kind of breakfast and lunch-focused and dinner for us is a sharing occasion. So perhaps that's why we're not seeing any impact, but we're going to continue to watch it. And with 34 million ways to make a pizza, we got a lot of choices out there. But if there needs to be menu innovation around that, we will do that.

Risks Reiterated

Especially given the potential Iran-driven inflationary pressure and the growing K-shaped economic disparity, PZZA may be forced into an even tougher position, having to scale down while also being required to focus on value and cost controls, which could even push the management and shareholders into considering the "easy way out" of a takeover.

Meanwhile, broader macro pressure can also affect their valuation, as the Fed's moves can affect their turnaround efforts and valuation, impacting the market's expectations and demands.

Valuation

For PZZA's DCF to Equity, I'll try to gauge the market's current expectations. Assuming no changes in net borrowings, with some benefits from cost savings and the guidance for interest reductions offset by higher CAPEX, let's assume $65 million in FCF in 2026, growing to $115 million by 2030 (same level as 2023) thanks to an asset-light expansion (especially internationally), right-sizing the portfolio and delivering significant cost savings over time alongside a macro recovery, with a 5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as they'd continue their international expansion with a stronger fundamental pivot, as well as a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a discount rate of 12% to take into account the higher risks and macro pressure, demanding a strong margin of safety, as well as 33.03 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report and 2026 guidance.

This way, we get to an equity value of $1.09 billion, for an intrinsic value of $32.93 per share, which is very close to the current $35.31 at the moment of writing this, which would imply a P/FCF multiple of ~10.8x in year 10.

Under a slightly more optimistic scenario, assuming that they reach $130 million in 2030 and the market recognizes their turnaround with a 10% discount rate, we get to about $47.64, which is, coincidentally, almost identical to Irth's latest offer, although I can see the potential for even better performance after the near-term turnaround pressure.

Conclusion

PZZA is a Buy, backed by an attractive valuation that implies a solid margin of safety, with significant potential from the company's ongoing turnaround efforts and pending takeover offer.

There are risks to consider since we're talking about a turnaround story in a tough consumer environment, but the risk-reward is currently in the investment's favor, in my opinion.

data.title: Nowhere To Hide... Except Maybe Treasury Floating Rate Notes
data.description:
By Kevin Flanagan

The Middle East war has proven to be a conundrum for the U.S. Treasury (UST) market. Typically, in times of geopolitical uncertainty, Treasuries are viewed as a safe-haven asset to turn to. However, the surge in energy prices has resulted in heightened inflation fears, and as a result, UST fixed coupon yields across the maturity spectrum have risen noticeably.

Figure 1: U.S. Treasury Yields

Prior to the Middle East war, UST yields, such as the 2- and 10-year notes, had been falling as concerns surrounding AI’s potential impact on software companies and attendant private credit exposures had produced a flight-to-quality bid. In fact, 2-year yields dropped to 3.37%, the lowest reading since 2022, while 10-year yields fell below the 4% threshold to 3.94%.

While the surge in energy prices has stoked inflation fears, other factors contributing to the sell-off include the reversal of prior ‘long’ positions and yield curve positioning trades, as well as potential liquidation by Persian Gulf nations to raise cash to pay for infrastructure repairs that have been damaged by Iranian missiles and drones.

As a result, 2- and 10-year yield levels had, at one point, risen as much as 60 bps and 50 bps, respectively. While renewed concerns have surfaced about higher energy prices becoming a ‘tax’ on the economy, rates are still considerably above their pre-war levels (see above graph).

Figure 2: UST 10-Year TIP Yield

The interesting aspect to the UST market sell-off has come from the inflation-adjusted sector, or TIPS. Once again, investors discovered that TIPS are not immune from increasing yields, even if the catalyst was arguably increasing inflation fears. Remember, TIPS are referenced to actual trailing CPI figures; they are not reset to a specific interest rate, like secured overnight financing rate (SOFR) or the three-month T-bill, to which Treasury Floating Rate Notes (FRNs) are tied (see below). In other words, investing in TIPS is not necessarily an ideal way to position your bond portfolio for rising rates.

The above graph clearly illustrates this point, as 10-year TIPS have seen their yield increase by +30bp from pre-war levels.

Potential Solution: Treasury FRNs

This is where Treasury FRNs can come into play. As investors have witnessed over the last four weeks or so, apart from the FRN space, there has been no place to hide along the UST yield curve, as short-, intermediate- and longer-dated maturities and TIPS have all seen yield levels rise in a visible fashion.

The March jobs report underscored our position that the Fed is at, or near the end, of this rate cut cycle. So, if the Fed is essentially done, or close to it, UST FRN yields are bottoming out. In addition, when the Middle East war does de-escalate, fixed coupon yields may come down from their high watermarks, but it could prove to be short-lived, and volatility is not going away any time soon.

The WisdomTree Floating Rate Treasury Fund (USFR) offers investors a potential solution to avoid uncertainty and volatility, as well as any potential increase in rates, while still providing a relatively attractive income opportunity.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Securities with floating rates can be less sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with fixed interest rates, but may decline in value. Fixed income securities will normally decline in value as interest rates rise. The value of an investment in the Fund may change quickly and without warning in response to issuer or counterparty defaults and changes in the credit ratings of the Fund’s portfolio investments. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Kevin Flanagan, Head of Investment and Fixed Income Strategy

Kevin serves as the Head of Investment and Fixed Income Strategy. In this role, he writes macro and fixed income-related content and works closely with the sales, research and marketing teams. In addition, Kevin conducts client-facing webinars and meetings, providing expertise on WisdomTree’s existing and future bond ETFs. Prior to joining WisdomTree, Kevin spent 30 years at Morgan Stanley, where he was Managing Director and Chief Fixed Income Strategist for Wealth Management. He was responsible for tactical and strategic recommendations and created asset allocation models for fixed income securities. He was a contributor to the Morgan Stanley Wealth Management Global Investment Committee, primary author of Morgan Stanley Wealth Management’s monthly and weekly fixed income publications, and collaborated with the firm’s Research and Consulting Group Divisions to build ETF and fund manager asset allocation models. Kevin has an MBA from Pace University’s Lubin Graduate School of Business and a B.S. in Finance from Fairfield University.

Original Post

data.title: Middle Eastern Banks: Tested By Conflict
data.description:
By Mariana Villalba, CFA

The ongoing conflict in Iran and its expansion across the region to target GCC countries unfolded following a period of debt-issuance growth in the region, especially from the financials sector.

As the conflict continues, we believe a deeper dive into GCC financials is warranted. Below, we take a closer look at GCC banks, which represent about one-tenth of the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified’s[1] market capitalization.

GCC Diversification From Oil to Credit

Over the past few years, Gulf GCC countries have been actively pursuing economic diversification away from the hydrocarbon sector and pursuing social transformation by attracting foreign human and financial capital into the region.

This is evidenced by the performance of non-hydrocarbon economic growth, which has outperformed real gross domestic product (GDP) growth in recent years, as the chart below shows.

Real GDP Growth of the Non-Oil Economy in GCC States (Year-Over-Year Change)

Source: IMF Database: Middle East and Central Asia Regional Economic Outlook.

Increased public debt issuance has accompanied economic diversification. The first chart below shows the Middle East’s share of emerging markets (EM) corporate primary debt issuance since 2017.

The second chart shows that as of December 2025, the Middle East made up nearly one-fifth of the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified’s market capitalization, of which GCC countries represented more than 80%. The financials sector accounted for about half of the region’s exposure.

EM Corporate Debt Issuance

Source: J.P. Morgan, as of December 2025. Universe is the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified. *Excludes 100% government-owned entities.

Regional Breakdown of the Index (Percentage of Market Capitalization)

Source: J.P. Morgan, as of December 31, 2025. Universe is the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified.

Increased debt issuance from the financials sector has driven the region’s issuance growth. As a result, the share of GCC financials in the index has risen by more than 2.5 percentage points to nearly 9% since 2021, as shown in the chart below. This growth was mainly driven by issuers in Saudi Arabia, UAE, and Kuwait.

GCC Market Cap (Financials vs. Other GCC Non-Financial Corporates)

Source: J.P. Morgan, as of December 31, 2025. Universe is the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified.

GCC Financials Breakdown by Country (Percentage of the Index)

Source: J.P. Morgan, as of December 31, 2025. Universe is the J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified.

How the Conflict May Affect GCC Banks

After years of stability that fostered a supportive operating environment, the ongoing conflict in Iran and its expansion across the region into GCC countries may pose challenges to the pillars of the region’s growth strategy and to banks in the region.

In our view, there are three main channels through which the conflict may impact banks in the region. The degree of impact may vary depending on each bank’s business model, level of diversification, and overall strategy.

Loan Growth. We believe the most immediate and direct impact of the conflict on the banking sector is its negative impact on economic activity due to physical disruption, infrastructure destruction, and lower consumer and corporate confidence.

This may impact banks’ lending growth and potentially revenue generation. From a credit investor’s perspective, however, a slowdown in lending growth might be positive from a capital perspective, although there are other factors that could offset that positive effect.

Funding. This is another potential channel of impact, both in terms of liquidity availability and cost of funding. As energy prices have risen, global rates have risen and financial conditions have tightened.

Both institutional investors and depositors may become more risk-averse and require a higher premium to provide funding to banks in the region. Additionally, the economic disruption caused by the conflict might reduce companies’ cash flows and hence the volume of deposits in the financial system.

Asset Quality. A slowdown in economic activity and damage to physical infrastructure may weaken the creditworthiness of borrowers and impact their ability to repay their debts, reduce the value of collateral, and even lead to defaults. This could entail additional provisioning requirements from banks and increase the balance of non-performing loans (NPLs).

While risks abound and the level of uncertainty is high, we believe GCC banks could benefit from strengths that allow them to navigate this turbulent period relatively well from a credit perspective. These include strong balance sheets, favorable ownership structures, and a supportive regulatory environment.

Strong Balance Sheets

GCC banks came to the conflict in a position of fundamental strength. The median NPL coverage of banks in the region was 150%, according to our analysis, which indicates that there is room to absorb some asset-quality deterioration.

At the same time, banks maintained a median capital buffer of more than 400 basis points above minimum requirements across the GCC,[2] and more than 300 basis points when excluding Saudi Arabia. That should alleviate concerns about solvency, even in the absence of extraordinary support.

One caveat pertains to dispersion. Individual banks may have weaker credit fundamentals than the aggregate data suggests and hence be more vulnerable to shocks. However, these banks represent a smaller part of our investment universe.

Supportive Ownership Structures

Most banks in the region have government ownership, often through controlling stakes. In turn, the creditworthiness of most GCC sovereigns is very strong, as evidenced by high credit ratings, relatively low debt levels, and high foreign exchange reserves.

Combined, these factors translate into a strong ability and willingness to support local banks. This is often explicitly reflected in banks’ credit ratings, with many benefiting from rating uplifts directly as a result of their ownership structure.

Supportive Regulatory Environment

In addition to the expectations of support embedded in the credit rating agencies’ assessments we described above, bank regulators and central banks in the region have a track record of providing support to the banking sector in events of stress. This happened during the COVID-19 period.

Following the start of the Iran conflict, regulators in the UAE, Qatar, and Kuwait came out with measures of support and/or relief for their respective banking sectors, with the UAE providing the most comprehensive plan.

These measures include, among others, 1) temporary relaxation of liquidity and capital requirements; 2) provision for additional liquidity facilities; 3) temporary flexibility for bad-loan recognition; 4) temporary easing of reserve requirements; and 5) payment relief for borrowers.

While we are not downplaying the economic and human cost of the conflict, which are significant and should not be neglected, we remain comfortable with the fundamentals of GCC banks, which should support relative resilience through this turbulent period.

[1] The J.P. Morgan CEMBI Broad Diversified is a widely used benchmark that tracks the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated corporate bonds issued by companies in EMs.

[2] Does not consider pillar 2 requirements applicable in Saudi Arabia, which are not publicly disclosed.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dave: Rapid Growth Through Members And AI Implementation
data.description:
Dave (DAVE) is a digital banking platform focused on improving financial access for everyday Americans, particularly those underserved by traditional banks. There is an increasing shift toward digital-first financial solutions via fintech and neobanks that offer speed, transparency, and lower costs versus legacy institutions. DAVE offers cash advance products, fee-free banking, and financial tools to consumers.

I gave DAVE a hold last August, with indications I really do like the stock as a longer-term play but just felt it was overvalued at the moment. The stock has dropped 8% since that time, which is nothing crazy, but the expansion in adjusted earnings over the last two quarters has been monumental and leaves the stock now trading at a big discount.

I have DAVE as a strong buy. 2025 results heavily exceeded expectations, and guidance for this year shows management confidence they will continue to boom. Members are up on all accounts, originations are climbing, their CashAI is helping them reduce credit risk, and value is now a strength to show investors.

Results and Guidance

Year-end 2025 data for DAVE blew their initial projections out of the water. They ended 2024 with $347 million in revenue and anticipated a roughly 20% increase, which resulted in a 60% uptick and $554 million in revenue. Adjusted EBITDA was even crazier, up 162% YoY. Core operations are running on all cylinders right now for DAVE, and even though the stock is up 133% in one year, I don’t think the share price is reflective of how smooth the company has been operationally. Cash from operations was up more than 2.3x in the last year and up almost 9x over the last two years. Another wild number is how they have managed to grow in this fashion while actually even lowering their total operating expenses from 2022 to 2025.

Management gave initial predictions for 2026 at about 27% growth for both sales and adjusted EBITDA. I think, based on their current efficiency and market growth, they will easily outpace this, but even a 27% gain would be nice. DAVE's EBITDA and net margin are both well above sector peers, and I would not be surprised to see 2026 year-end adjusted EBITDA closer to $350 million.

Member Growth

The obvious driver behind all of this is simply their member growth. Total members have consistently risen, which is a good sign showing it’s not just cyclical patterns from consumers. Transacting members are also up 19% YoY as engagement within their member base proves strong. Spend volumes are up in pattern with member growth, as management notes that there has been fantastic synergy with their ExtraCash segment. On top of retaining and building their member base, Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) has rocketed up 36% on the year, as they mention ExtraCash optimization and the rollout of a $3 monthly subscription fee for all new members in Q2 last year.

Originations Strengthening

Origination volume grew 50% for DAVE last year, driven by tapping further into the addressable market for this industry and also a reflection on better approval limits with the help of their CashAI product. More originations just lead to more fees collected and signal higher customer demand. The $2.2 billion in originations last year led to a $297 million net receivables balance at year-end.

I don’t see a lot of risk at the moment for this stock, but here goes my “risk” section. With higher origination volume comes higher potential for greater loan default, which is a risk. DAVE's delinquency rate, which is the number of loans that are past due, has dropped for 2 straight quarters as CashAI has officially been implemented and is efficiently doing its thing as the firm’s own AI-powered underwriting system. Credit risk is always going to be out there in the finance sector, especially when consumers are uneasy and allocating less toward savings, which has been the case in recent years. DAVE has taken the risk into their own hands and has found a way to lower the credit risk by building an internal system.

What is CashAI?

DAVE describes CashAI v 5.5 as its own “proprietary AI-driven cash flow underwriting engine.” The system analyzes real-time bank account data, looking at customers income, spending, and other patterns. It uses patterns from billions of transactions and re-checks consumer risk after every time they borrow, unlike traditional models. CashAI can then limit or stop the client spending and steer DAVE away from higher-risk users that have a better chance to default. Since v5.5 was enrolled, delinquency rates and past-due rates have dropped over the last six months. CashAI v6.0 development is well underway, and they expect even further optimization in late 2026. This has arguably been the biggest component to the rapid financial growth DAVE is seeing on profitability and hedge to consumer credit risk.

Valuation

Valuation was the biggest reason I didn’t have DAVE as a buy last time I wrote in August. PE at the time was north of 50x and well above the sector median. It seemed like either the stock was overvalued and needed a correction, or a huge earnings expansion was needed. The latter happened and also happened very fast. The stock now has a PE of 12.7x, right in line with the industry, and is sitting at a major discount compared to the majority of the last year. This is also supported by an incredibly low PEG ratio of 0.06, about 10x lower than the sector median. The big piece keeping me from a buy last time has evaporated and now even looks discounted.

Conclusion

I really don’t see anything not to like about DAVE. They are steadily increasing their member base through intelligent operational success and a strong addressable market. From 2021 to now, consumers living paycheck to paycheck have increased from 57% to 66%. The economy is unsteady, and DAVE is capturing consumers looking to get out of traditional banking options. CashAI has been a vital piece to the growth and seems to be the biggest difference maker and what separates them from fintech peers and traditional institutions. Their customer acquisition cost is incredibly low, and operating expenses are relatively flat. 2025 sales and adjusted EBITDA were knocked out of the park, and the 2026 forecast projects the same. I believe in this neobank industry based on Gen Z patterns, and DAVE is the leader. DAVE is a strong buy.

data.title: Fastly: The Quant Score Says Buy, The Fundamentals Say Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fastly, Inc. (FSLY) is a San Francisco-based edge cloud platform. The stock did very well in FY2025, surging to highs of ~$35 for a roughly 600% move from its 52-week low. Last year was the company's first non-GAAP profitable fiscal year, with revenue of $624 million (up 15% YoY) and guidance for FY2026 of $700-$720 million in revenue and $50-$60 million in non-GAAP operating income. Net revenue retention climbed to 110%. RPO grew 55% YoY to $353.8 million. The cybersecurity business grew 21%. After years of negative cash flow and investor worries about customer concentration, Fastly finally seems to have turned the corner.

But there is something that gives us pause: the current valuation. At about 8.3x trailing P/S, Fastly trades at a meaningful premium to Akamai (AKAM) (~4x) while growing three times faster, but at a steep discount to Cloudflare (NET) (~27x forward P/S), which grows twice as fast. Fastly's value likely falls between the two comps. The market is betting on the Cloudflare-type arc. We are not convinced. AI Accelerator and semantic caching must prove they can move the revenue needle. Until they do, we see the risk-reward as balanced. We rate Fastly a HOLD.

What Fastly Does

Fastly operates a global edge cloud platform. It runs a distributed network of servers at the perimeter of the internet, pushing content delivery, security enforcement, and increasing computation as close to end users as possible. If a user in Tokyo loads a web page, Fastly's edge node in Tokyo serves it, not a data center in Virginia.

Network services represented $477.8 million of FY2025 revenue, or about 78% of the total. Security products, including web application firewall, DDoS protection, and bot management, contributed $125.1 million (20%). A smaller "other" category covers "emerging compute" and AI products and added $21.1 million, up 64% YoY. That last bucket is small but worth watching.

Fastly started as a content delivery network (CDN) company built for developers. That focus has shifted. Now the company leads with Compute@Edge, a serverless platform based on WebAssembly (Wasm) that lets customers run software at the network edge with decreased latency and increased efficiency. The 2026 growth strategy leans heavily on AI Accelerator, a semantic caching layer meant to cut the cost of running large language model inference at the network edge. Finding new customers, or at least converting existing customers at scale, will drive the investment case over the next 12-18 months.

As of Q4 2025, no single customer exceeds 10% of revenue. Total customer count stood at 3,092, with 628 enterprise customers spending an average of roughly $900,000 annually. That is up from 596 enterprise customers a year earlier.

Financials

FY2025 was a great year for Fastly.

Revenue hit $624 million, up 15% from $543.7 million in FY2024. The final months saw continued momentum; Q4 revenue grew 23% YoY, reaching $172.6 million, the company's fastest quarterly growth rate in years.

Margins improved as well; GAAP gross margin hit 61.4% in Q4, showing an increase in higher-margin security and compute offerings.

Fastly reported $19.7 million in non-GAAP net income for FY2025, its first full year of positive non-GAAP earnings. The prior year showed a $12.1 million non-GAAP loss. FY2026 guidance of $50-$60 million in non-GAAP operating income shows continued margin expansion is expected.

Despite improvement, Fastly still reported a GAAP net loss of $121.7 million for FY2025. Stock-based compensation of $117.3 million constitutes the majority of the deviation between GAAP and non-GAAP results. Until GAAP profitability is achieved, the company cannot technically be considered profitable.

Operating cash flow reached $94.4 million, reversing years of negative or sparse cash flow. After $28.7 million in capex, free cash flow was about $65.7 million. R&D spend was $162.7 million, or ~26% of revenue.

The company ended FY2025 with $180.6 million in cash and equivalents and $181.2 million in short-term investments. Total debt stood at $430.2 million, of which $323.3 million is long-term. That puts net debt at $68.4 million and the Debt/Equity ratio at 0.46.

Valuation

This is where our concern lies. Shares currently trade around $34, giving the company a market cap of roughly $5.2 billion. The most recent surge was driven by the appointment of marketing executive Joan Jenkins as CMO. The 52-week low was $4.87, so shares have appreciated over 600% and now sit at the 52-week high of $34.82. SA's aggregate Wall St. analyst score shows a 3.50/5 Buy rating, SA analysts rate it 3.83/5 Buy, and the SA Quant rating is Strong Buy at 4.99/5. That discrepancy between quant momentum and fundamentals tells us that technical traders love this stock, but the analysts covering it have not revised their models to account for the recent rally.

At current levels, the valuations are stretched. Trailing P/S is ~8.3x. EV/Sales (TTM) is ~8.4x, and forward EV/Sales is ~7.3x based on consensus FY2026 revenue of $712 million. Investors are paying for accelerated growth and an AI edge vertical that has not been proven.

Peer Comparison

Company Mkt Cap EV FY25 Rev Rev Growth Gross Margin EV/Rev (TTM) Fwd P/S
Akamai (AKAM) $17.4B $21.1B $4.21B 5% ~60% ~5x ~4x
Cloudflare (NET) $74.5B $73.9B $2.17B 30% 75% ~34x ~27x
Fastly (FSLY) $5.2B $5.2B $624M 15% 57% 8.3x 7.1x

Fastly sits in the uncomfortable middle ground between two very different peers, each representing a compelling story for investors.

Cloudflare is the growth story, with $2.17 billion in FY2025 revenue, 30% growth, 75% gross margins, and a market cap 15x the size of Fastly. The valuation gap is wide (~27x forward P/S versus Fastly's ~7x), but Cloudflare's scale, growth rate, and customer base justify the premium. Cloudflare is already building the type of platform that Fastly aspires to become.

Akamai is the value story, with $4.21 billion in revenue, strong profitability, and a forward P/E of ~17x. Akamai trades at roughly 4x forward P/S. The market sees Akamai as a mature, commoditizing business. Fastly's 7x P/S multiple implies investors see it as closer to Cloudflare than Akamai, but 15% revenue growth does not quite justify that view.

Market consensus prices Fastly as a high-growth edge-AI software play, not a commodity CDN vendor. At its current growth rate, that remains aspirational.

The Bull Case

Network-edge AI is what currently drives the stock price. Fastly's AI Accelerator, slated for wider rollout this fiscal year, uses semantic caching to cut the cost of running LLM inference at the edge. Enterprises could choose edge-based inference for savings in cost or latency over centralized inference. Fastly has a head start with its global PoP footprint and Wasm-based compute. The issue is that nobody has proven the market exists at scale yet.

The more concrete growth lever is security. The segment grew 21% in FY2025 and now makes up 20% of revenue. AI Bot Management, launched in 2025, targets a real and growing problem for enterprises dealing with AI scrapers. This product is scalable, has real growth potential, and should help expand margins.

Net revenue retention at 110% in Q4 2025, up from 106% in Q3, shows that existing customers are spending more. RPO growth of 55% YoY to $353.8 million shows strong forward visibility. Enterprise customer count grew 5% to 628. Revenue from the company’s ten largest customers increased 28%, a positive signal for customer satisfaction.

Forrester named Fastly a Leader in edge development platforms in Q1 2026, which should help with enterprise sales cycles. The company's Compute@Edge platform, built on Wasm, offers near-zero cold starts that AWS Lambda@Edge cannot match. If Wasm adoption accelerates in the developer community, Fastly's early bet on the technology could pay off.

Risks

The stock trades at ~$34 at the 52-week high of $34.82, and analysts do not think there is much upside left. SA's quant rating aggregator gives the Valuation factor a D grade, even as it rates the overall stock a Strong Buy on momentum and growth. That tension between technicals and fundamentals is the single largest risk in our view.

Non-GAAP profitability is a big accomplishment for Fastly, but the GAAP accounting reflects a net loss of $121.7 million for FY2025. For investors who care about GAAP earnings (they should), Fastly is still a GAAP net-loss operation, with a $5 billion market cap. A company trading at 8x sales needs to be speeding up, not slowing down. One guidance miss in 2026 would compress the multiple fast.

Cloudflare's revenue is 3.5x Fastly's and growing at 30% versus Fastly's 15%. Both companies compete for enterprise edge and security budgets. Cloudflare's larger scale and higher margins give it more room to invest in R&D, undercut competitors, and bundle services. In an environment where Fastly and Cloudflare are competing for the same business, Cloudflare currently has the advantage. Fastly's main advantage is developer experience and Wasm performance, but that advantage will likely narrow over time.

AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN, and Google Cloud CDN are sufficient for most customers and often come included with or heavily discounted as part of larger cloud infrastructure contracts. Fastly's niche is a differentiator, but many enterprises that are committed to a hyperscaler have little incentive to add a standalone CDN vendor.

AI Accelerator and semantic caching sound great in theory, but the question we struggle with is, will enterprises actually route LLM inference through Fastly when they could run it centrally through OpenAI, Anthropic, or their hyperscaler of choice? If the AI product line does not deliver, Fastly remains a 15% YoY growth CDN and security operation trading at nearly 8x sales.

Conclusion

Fastly's management has done a great job turning the business around. Operating cash flow hit $94.4 million, gross margins are expanding, and the business is nearing GAAP profitability. Diversification of their client base and revenue mix is a welcome development, and the security vertical is growing.

Our hesitation comes down to fundamentals. The 600%+ rally from the 52-week low has left the stock trading in risky territory. At $34, the market cap is well ahead of the underlying business. A company growing revenue at ~15% and generating $19.7 million in non-GAAP profit does not typically command a $5 billion market cap unless the market sees an acceleration that the Street has not yet modeled. Also worth noting, the FY2026 growth strategy leans heavily on AI Accelerator. While the product has long-term potential, it is still unproven and lacks a clear, scalable market in the near term.

At these prices, we cannot justify the underlying valuation. For now, we have to wait and see how things shake out from the sidelines. If Q2 and Q3 show AI Accelerator generating revenue, and the growth rate pushes above 20%, current multiples become more tenable.

At Avalon Capital Research, we rate Fastly, Inc. a HOLD.

data.title: Fidelity Limited Term Municipal Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Limited Term Municipal Income Fund (FSTFX) is a diversified national municipal bond strategy investing in general obligation and revenue- or tax-backed municipal securities across the short-intermediate part of the yield curve.
  • Our investment approach focuses on fundamental credit analysis, yield-curve positioning and an analysis of the structural characteristics of each security.
  • The fund's interest rate sensitivity is targeted closely to that of its benchmark to prevent interest rate speculation from overwhelming research-based strategies that we deem to have a higher likelihood of long-term success.
  • We emphasize a total-return approach that seeks to generate a high level of tax-exempt income, consistent with the preservation of capital.

Performance Summary

Morningstar Fund Muni National Short 0.67% 3.86% 3.86% 3.43% 1.52% 1.58%

1 Life of Fund (LOF) if performance is less than 10 years. Fund inception date: 12/24/1986.

2 This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund. It does not include any fee waivers or reimbursements, which would be reflected in the fund's net expense ratio.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate; therefore, you may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance stated. Performance shown is that of the fund's Retail Class shares (if multiclass). You may own another share class of the fund with a different expense structure and, thus, have different returns. To learn more or to obtain the most recent month-end or other share-class performance, visit Fidelity Funds | Mutual Funds from Fidelity Investments, Financial Professionals | Fidelity Institutional, or 401k.com. Total returns are historical and include change in share value and reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any. Cumulative total returns are reported as of the period indicated.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Fund Information

Manager(s):

Team Managed

Trading Symbol:

FSTFX

Start Date:

December 24, 1986

Size (in millions):

$2,840.56

Morningstar Category:

Fund Muni National Short

The municipal market can be affected by adverse tax, legislative, or political changes and the financial condition of the issuers of municipal securities. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Income exempt from federal income tax may be subject to state or local tax. All or a portion of the fund's income may be subject to the federal alternative minimum tax. Income or fund distributions attributable to capital gains are usually subject to both state and federal income taxes. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Not FDIC Insured • May Lose Value • No Bank Guarantee

Municipal Market Review

Tax-exempt municipal bonds capped off 2025 with a resilient fourth quarter, sustaining the momentum they gained in the prior three months as interest rates declined, tax-backed credit fundamentals remained solid, and demand ticked higher. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index gained 1.56% (including interest payments and price changes) for the quarter, helping munis offset a volatile first half of 2025 and lifting their full-year advance to 4.25%.

Munis notched most of their Q4 gain in October (+1.24%). That month, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced its benchmark policy interest rate by a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), marking the central bank's second such rate cut in as many months, aimed at supporting a softening job market.

Improving technical factors were an additional tailwind. On the supply side, the pace of new bond issuance decelerated. Meanwhile, demand for munis strengthened, particularly among investors looking to lock in yields ahead of further anticipated interest-rate cuts. Moreover, passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July preserved the tax-exempt status of municipal bonds, removing a headwind that had created uncertainty in the muni market during the first half of 2025.

Munis then largely held steady in November (+0.23%) and December (+0.09%), supported by another quarter-point Federal Reserve rate reduction in December. Although new-issue volume remained elevated and contributed to a record year for issuance, it slowed toward year-end amid the typical seasonal lull in new deals. Supply was largely absorbed without market disruption amid firming investor demand, as healthy yields continued to attract buyers' attention.

Credit fundamentals for most tax-backed munis remained solid throughout the three months, with default rates very low and issuers' balance sheets generally healthy.

For the full quarter, long-duration municipals led the market, with 10-, 15-, 20- and 22-plus-year bonds posting the top gains (+1.74%, +2.84%, +2.20% and 1.63%, respectively), while one-, three-, five- and seven-year munis advanced 0.56%, 0.46%, 0.50% and 0.83%, respectively.

From a sector perspective, revenue bonds (+1.57%) performed roughly in line with general obligation bonds (+1.60%). Among GOs, local-government debt (+1.71%) modestly outpaced state-backed bonds (+1.44%). In the revenue category, there was little differentiation between the performance of various segments or ratings. Housing (+1.77%) and hospital bonds (+1.75%) fared best, while most other sectors followed closely behind – except for industrial development bonds, which returned only 0.65%.

By credit-quality rating, BBB-rated bonds gained 1.77%, slightly bettering returns in the 1.50% range notched by munis with higher ratings (AAA, AA, A).

THREE-MONTH MUNI INDEX RETURNS BY CREDIT QUALITY

Quality Total Return

AA 1.57%
A 1.50%
BAA 1.77%
Index 1.56%

Returns represent those of the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index.

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Sector Allocation
Market Environment General obligation bonds and revenue bonds posted similar returns.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark) Security selection among airport and health care bonds contributed to performance versus the Bloomberg 1-6 Year Municipal Bond Index. (Positive)
Strategy: Yield Curve
Market Environment The muni yield curve flattened during the quarter, with the middle and long end of the curve shifting down, and the short end shifting up.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark) The fund's yield-curve positioning contributed to relative performance. (Positive)
Strategy: Credit
Market Environment Credit yield spreads were largely unchanged across rating categories.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark) A higher average yield on the fund's underlying holdings helped it gain more carry than the index. (Positive)
Strategy: Additional Factors
Market Environment The fund and its benchmark employ somewhat different methodologies in estimating the prices of municipal securities, most of which trade infrequently.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark) Pricing methodology differences modestly boosted relative performance. (Positive)

Outlook and Positioning

As of December 31, yields on municipal bonds are still relatively high historically, offering tax-equivalent yields that compare favorably with U.S. Treasuries and corporate bonds, particularly for investors in high tax brackets.

As for market technicals, we believe new-issue supply may continue to remain high as issuers increase their borrowing due to higher costs and uncertainty around future federal funding. Against that backdrop, we believe investor demand will need to remain strong to absorb this higher level of expected issuance.

It is important for investors to remember that Fidelity's municipal bond portfolios are constructed with a careful and intentional emphasis on issue selection, especially with consideration to the liquidity of the security and the financial resiliency of its issuer. In the current environment, we continue to evaluate each fund holding and are monitoring those that may be more financially challenged than others.

Going forward, and as always, we will seek to identify opportunities to purchase municipal securities that offer both attractive valuations and a solid risk/reward profile. At quarter end, we continue to invest for strong risk-adjusted returns, while remaining cautious with yield-curve positioning, given the likelihood of ongoing interest-rate volatility.

We remain committed to the approach of building individual exposures in the portfolio that reflect risks with which we are comfortable, at entry prices we believe offer strong relative value. ■

Definitions and Important Information

Unless otherwise expressly disclosed to you in writing, the information provided in this material is for educational purposes only. Any viewpoints expressed by Fidelity are not intended to be used as a primary basis for your investment decisions and are based on facts and circumstances at the point in time they are made and are not particular to you. Accordingly, nothing in this material constitutes impartial investment advice or advice in a fiduciary capacity, as defined or under the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 or the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, both as amended. Fidelity and its representatives may have a conflict of interest in the products or services mentioned in this material because they have a financial interest in the products or services and may receive compensation, directly or indirectly, in connection with the management, distribution, and/or servicing of these products or services, including Fidelity funds, certain third-party funds and products, and certain investment services. Before making any investment decisions, you should take into account all of the particular facts and circumstances of your or your client's individual situation and reach out to a professional adviser, if applicable.

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

30-day SEC Tax-Equivalent Yield shows what you would have to earn on a taxable investment to equal the fund's tax-free yield, if you are in the 37% effective federal income tax bracket and also subject to the 3.8% Medicare Contribution tax, but does not reflect the payment of the federal alternative minimum tax, if applicable. Medicare Contribution tax is a tax on non-municipal investment income that applies to individuals with incomes over $200,000 (or $250,000, filing jointly). For state-specific funds, TEY is based not only on the highest federal tax rate (40.8%) but also the highest state tax rate. For state-specific funds, TEYs assume investors are state residents and would not be able to take an itemized deduction on their federal returns for state taxes on investment income. For NY funds, TEYs do not reflect the NY state tax rate that applies to income in excess of $5 million. For MD funds, TEYs reflect the highest city/county tax rates in MD and treat them the same as state taxes. Consult a tax professional for further detail.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund;

determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

Subject to Alternative Minimum Tax is the percent of interest income subject to the alternative minimum tax (AMT) based on total net assets within a portfolio.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (index) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Bloomberg 1-6 Year Municipal Bond Index is a market-value-weighted index of investment-grade fixed-rate municipal bonds with maturities between one and six years.

Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a market value-weighted index of investment-grade municipal bonds with maturities of one year or more.

LIPPER INFORMATION

Lipper Averages are averages of the performance of all mutual funds with their respective investment classification category. The number of funds in each category periodically changes. Lipper, a Refinitiv company, is a nationally recognized organization that ranks the performance of mutual funds.

MORNINGSTAR INFORMATION

SECTOR WEIGHTS

Sector weights illustrate examples of market segments in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any subset of the market.

Before investing in any mutual fund, please carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses. For this and other information, call or write Fidelity for a free prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus. Read it carefully before you invest.

data.title: Rigel Pharmaceuticals Was Easy To Dismiss - Now It's Hard To Ignore (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

My past hesitation in Rigel Pharmaceuticals (RIGL) was twofold: financial and operational.

Financially, Rigel was a microcap stock with a dwindling cash reserve. Operationally, Tavalisse seemed no more than a niche product. And although Rezlidhia had just launched, it didn’t appear to have a place in AML.

However, I did acknowledge some signs a turnaround. Losses were narrowing and partnerships with Eli Lilly (LLY) de-risked pipeline aspirations.

I ended with a cautious “Hold” rating.

RIGL has since gone up 140%.

I take another look below.

Recent Developments

After acquiring the US rights from Gavreto from Blueprint Medicines in early 2024, Rigel now makes money from three products. Gavreto, FDA-approved for both RET+ metastatic NSCLC and RET+ advanced thyroid cancer, generated $42.1M in FY25. Meanwhile, Rigel’s legacy products, Tavalisse and Rezlidhia, brought in $158.8M and $31M, respectively.

Although revenues were up 64% YoY in FY25, profitability did not suffer. Rigel's gross profit margin (82.02%) and EBITDA margin (43.46%) outperform its peers in the Health Care sector. And, in the trailing twelve months, Rigel generated $75.7M in cash from operations.

This is likely attributed to the "tuck-in" nature of Gavreto. Rigel's existing salesforce already had plenty of experience with hematologists/oncologists. So it ended up fitting right in without much added cost.

Moving on to pipeline developments, R289 is currently in a Phase 1b study for lower-risk MDS. Rigel presented encouraging data at ASH last year. 33% (6/18) of transfusion-dependent patients achieved transfusion independence, or TI, on R289.

Ocadusertib (Eli Lilly), a RIPK1 inhibitor, recently completed enrollment for the Phase 2 study in rheumatoid arthritis. Eli Lilly did exit the CNS arm of its Rigel collaboration last year.

Gavreto

Gavreto has become a “preferred” first-line option in the NCCN Guidelines for RET fusion-positive metastatic NSCLC.

Eli Lilly's Retevmo, Gavreto's primary competitor, maintains slightly higher efficacy numbers in untreated patients (cross-trial limitations aside). But Gavreto, with a 38.8 month median DOR, appears to have an edge in pre-treated patients.

RET fusion-positive only accounts for ~1-2% of total NSCLC cases, but it’s safe to assume that Gavreto’s peak share in this niche market could be much larger than Blueprint expected when they out-licensed it.

My discounted cash flow model (Gavreto in NSCLC) assumes 2031 Peak, 2036 LOE, 2,050 Target Pop., 30% Peak Share, 0.5% annual Pop. Growth, 80% Ownership Split, $180k Net Price, 10% COGS, 25% Steady-State OpEx, and a Competitor Impact of 10% beginning in 2028 (potential impact from next-gen RET inhibitors).

I have Gavreto generating a little over $80M in peak annual revenue. It is worth roughly $245M in Net Present Value.

Rezlidhia & Tavalisse

Tavalisse’s demand has exploded in ITP, although policy tailwinds (i.e., the elimination of the Medicare Part D coverage gap) in FY25, expected to subside in FY26, played some part.

Tavalisse is typically viewed as one of four options in second-line treatment for ITP. It is particularly popular among patients who want to avoid immunosuppression (i.e., rituximab) or surgery (i.e., splenectomy), which is more people than you'd think.

Recent real-world data highlighted Tavalisse’s effectiveness in elderly and comorbid patients who cannot tolerate the thrombotic risks associated with TPO-RAs (the third “other” second-line treatment option).

Tavalisse has carved a niche in ITP and figures to remain a steady contributor.

Rezlidhia is dominated by Tibsovo in IDH1 AML. Rigel is hoping to expand Rezlidhia into glioma (Phase 2) and front-line combos.

Pipeline

R289’s 33% TI rate may not sound like much, but it’s pretty impressive when you consider the population (salvage).

Dose-expansion data, expected later this year, hopes to, at the least, maintain this 30%+ TI rate.

R289 is the first IRAK1/4 inhibitor to show meaningful activity in MDS. Because it targets a unique pathway (the innate immune system), it may be a good candidate for combination therapy in the future, which would push it into earlier lines.

R289 comes with two major competitive advantages over competitors like SC Reblozyl and IV Rytelo:

  1. It is an oral pill.
  2. It has shown a manageable safety profile (Grade 1/2 GI effects).

When I model R289, its risk-adjusted NPV falls near 0. So, I suspect that further de-risking is needed (i.e., advance to Phase 2/3 trial; combination studies) for the stock to really begin to feel its impact.

FY26 Guidance

Rigel ended FY25 with a healthy $155M in cash and investments.

Rigel guides for between $275M and $290M in FY26 revenue. The FY26 guidance requires nuance, though.

After accounting for the $40M Rigel booked last year in non-cash revenue from the collaboration adjustment, management’s midpoint still calls for 12% organic revenue growth.

Because R289 is still years away from a launch, especially as management is carefully enrolling patients (slow and steady), it’s apparent that Rigel’s business could use a boost.

During the call, management expressed a desire for a new late-stage asset by 2028. So I think they’re feeling some pressure to sign a deal this year to ensure they have a new launch in the 2027/2028 window, lest revenue growth stall.

Rigel also signaled (“we’re not looking at expanding the sales organization at this point in time,” “very targeted efforts”) that they are more interested in squeezing every penny from a “Gavreto dollar” than they are expanding its reach.

Conclusion

Overall, I like what I am seeing from Rigel. I think they have some flexibility with regard to chasing growth and/or improving margins.

Gavreto has really paid off. And it increases confidence in management’s ability to strike gold again. Meanwhile, R289 and the Eli Lilly partnership keep things interesting “under the hood.” And now that Rigel is cash generative with a few established products, its stock does not feel as risky as it did before.

So I’m comfortable with upgrading my rating on RIGL to a speculative “Buy.”

Risks to investment include Tavalisse overdependence, competition, clinical trial failures, pricing pressures, regulatory headwinds, and management missteps.

This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.
